coredata.dc:title: Determination of potassium ferrocyanide in table salt and salted food using a water-soluble fluorescent silicon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Na, Min
Chen, Yonglei
Han, Yangxia
Ma, Sudai
Liu, Juanjuan
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: The addition of potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) in table salt as anti-caking agent has a crucial role in preventing the formation of lumps. However, the excess of K4[Fe(CN)6] and its decomposers are harmful to both human health and environment. To date, there are still lack of suitable methods for simple and rapid analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food. Herein, a novel fluorescent Si QDs probe for sensitive, selective and rapid detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] was synthesized by facile one-step strategy. Notably, the fluorescence of Si QDs could be remarkably quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] via electrostatic interaction. Based on this phenomenon, a new method of determination of K4[Fe(CN)6] was established. A wide linear range was obtained from 0.05 to 8.0 µg/mL with a detection limit of 30 ng/mL. The established fluorescent new method was suitable for detecting K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium ferrocyanide
Silicon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Table salt and salted food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of parameters used in the synthesis of the Si QDs
Optimization of detection conditions for K4[Fe(CN)6]
Fluorescent response property of the Si QDs toward K4[Fe(CN)6]
Selectivity of the Si QDs for K4[Fe(CN)6] detection
Possible FL response mechanism of the Si QDs to K4[Fe(CN)6]
Analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in actual samples
Semi-quantitative analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] by mobile phone
Other application of the Si QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) Diameter distribution, (C) EDS pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of the Si QDs.

High resolution XPS spectra of the Si QDs: (A) full range, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) Si 2p, respectively. (F) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (a), emission spectrum (b), and UV–vis absorption spectrum (c) of the Si QDs solution. (Insert: photographs of the Si QDs solutions under visible light and UV light with excitation wavelength at 365 nm, respectively).

(A) Fluorescence response of the Si QDs upon addition of various concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6] (from top to bottom, 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 12, and 14 μg/mL) in a pH 2.0 B-R solution. (B) F 0 / F of the Si QDs as a function of concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6]. F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the Si QDs in the absence and presence of K4[Fe(CN)6], respectively. Error bars are the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (C) Fluorescence quenching efficiency F 0/F of the Si QDs towards K4[Fe(CN)6] and other interferents (10-fold concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6], except for
Hg
2 +
,
Pb
2 +
, which are 1-fold concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6]). (D) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of the Si QDs towards K4[Fe(CN)6] and other interferents in the presence of K4[Fe(CN)6]. All experiments were conducted in 0.04 M B-R pH 2.0 buffer solution. Error bars are the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
Preparation Procedure of the Si QDs and Schematic Illustration of Prepared Si QDs for Specific Determination of K4[Fe(CN)6].
Analytical results for K4[Fe(CN)6] detection in different table salt samples.

row.entry: Algae iodate salt with calcium contained

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] in different salted food samples.

head.ce:title: Determination of potassium ferrocyanide in table salt and salted food using a water-soluble fluorescent silicon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China (X. Chen).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Department of Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The addition of potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) in table salt as anti-caking agent has a crucial role in preventing the formation of lumps. However, the excess of K4[Fe(CN)6] and its decomposers are harmful to both human health and environment. To date, there are still lack of suitable methods for simple and rapid analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food. Herein, a novel fluorescent Si QDs probe for sensitive, selective and rapid detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] was synthesized by facile one-step strategy. Notably, the fluorescence of Si QDs could be remarkably quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] via electrostatic interaction. Based on this phenomenon, a new method of determination of K4[Fe(CN)6] was established. A wide linear range was obtained from 0.05 to 8.0 µg/mL with a detection limit of 30 ng/mL. The established fluorescent new method was suitable for detecting K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) is an anti-caking agent (E536) which prevents food from lumping, denaturation and allows food granules (powders) flow freely (Li, Liu, Xie, Kong, & Liu, 2006). Therefore, K4[Fe(CN)6] is widely used as anti-caking agent in table salt. It is disadvantageous for physical health when excessive amount of K4[Fe(CN)6] is added to table salt (Li, Liu, Xie, Kong, & Liu, 2007). According to the report, when K4[Fe(CN)6] is inhaled or ingested can cause hypotoxicity and irritation to respiratory system, skin and eyes. It can even cause cough, shortness of breath and gastrointestinal discomfort. Moreover, under high temperature, even low level of K4[Fe(CN)6] produces potassium cyanide, which is converted into virulent hydrocyanic acid/sodium cyanide in acidic/alkaline condition (Burdick & Lipschuetz, 1950; Li et al., 2007). To ensure the safety and quality of table salt and salted food, governments have set maximum residue limits for K4[Fe(CN)6] in the table salt industry (The amount allowed is 10 mg/kg by China Food and Drug Administration, 2014; The amount allowed is 14 mg/kg by Codex Alimentarius Commission of FAO/WHO, 2014) and the LD50 for rat oral is 1.6–3.2 g/kg (bw) and ADI (acceptable daily intake) is 0–0.025 mg/kg (FAO/WHO, 1994) (Li et al., 2007). Considering the safety of food and the need of food import and export, it is vitally necessary to detect K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food with high sensitivity and accuracy.

Until now, the analytical methods for K4[Fe(CN)6] detection mainly include titrimetric methods, gravimetric methods, spectrophotometric methods (Avron & Shavit, 1963; Kidby, 1969), Resonance Rayleigh Scattering (RRS) methods (Li et al., 2006, 2007) and Thin-Layer Chromatography (TLC) methods. Unfortunatly, there are still some defects in the existing methods of analyzing K4[Fe(CN)6], such as time-consuming, expensive and complex instruments are needed, complicated post-processing procedures. Above all, most of the materials used in those methods are highly toxic to environment. Recently, fluorescence sensing has attracted wide attention due to its simple operation, high sensitivity and low cost. As far as we know, the detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] by fluorescent method has not yet been reported. Accordingly, it is urgently needed to explore an time-saving, facile, and environmental-friendly fluorescent approach for rapid analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in table salt and salted food with high sensitivity and selectivity.

It is worth noting that silicon-based nanostructure materials have brought considerable attention in recent years, owing to the abundant raw materials and the superb optical, electronic, mechanical, and catalytical properties (Song et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015). Meanwhile, silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) have been extensively employed in sensing and bioimaging fields due to they possess many unique superiority, such as wonderful water-solubility, good stability, low photobleaching rate, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, and biodegradability (Chu et al., 2016; Das, Saha, Maity, Ray, & Jana, 2013; Ma et al., 2016; Park et al., 2009; Zhong et al., 2013, 2015). Compared to the conventional fluorescent dye, the water-solubility of fluorescent Si QDs make them a suitable fit to determine K4[Fe(CN)6] in aqueous, the low photobleaching rate of fluorescent Si QDs ensures the accuracy of determination result of K4[Fe(CN)6], and low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility, biodegradability of fluorescent Si QDs guarantee method should be environmentally friendly. Therefore, fluorescent Si QDs more suitable for rapid and accurate determination microcomponent in the food, environmental and biological field.

In this paper, fluorescent and water-soluble Si QDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyldimethoxymethylsilane (DAMO) as silicon source and sodium ascorbate (AS) as reducing agent, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The resulting Si QDs exhibited good thermostability, strong photostability, excellent salt stability and water solubility. Of particular note, the fluorescence of as-prepared Si QDs could be quenched by K4[Fe(CN)6] (Scheme 1). Based on this phenomenon, a selective, sensitive and rapid method for the analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] was established. This method has been successfully used to determine the amount of K4[Fe(CN)6] in practical samples. Moreover, to achieve rapid detection of K4[Fe(CN)6], a method that allows convenient readout the concentration in samples was established by combining the Si QDs probe with a mobile phone. Furthermore, we observed that the Si QDs can also act as fluorescent nanosensors to monitor the pH change in wide range.

The Si QDs were synthesized by simple one-step strategy. Firstly, 2.3 g of AS was dissolved with 8.0 mL of ultrapure water and 2.0 mL of DAMO was mixed with the above AS solution in 80 °C water bath. Then, by stirring for 8 h, the color of reaction solution changed from pale yellow to yellowish-brown and to dark brown at last, which indicated the generation of the Si QDs. Finally, the as prepared Si QDs solution was cooled to room temperature and the overplus reactants were removed by dialysis (1 kDa, molecular weight cutoff) for about 5 h. After purifying the Si QDs solution, it was kept at 4 °C for the following experiments.

The stock solution of K4[Fe(CN)6] was produced as follow: 0.2050 g of K4[Fe(CN)6] were dissolved into 50.0 mL of ultrapure water. The stock solution was diluted to prepare various concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6]. The obtained Si QDs were diluted 6000-fold with 0.04 M Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solution (pH 2.0) for the determination of K4[Fe(CN)6]. Briefly: 100 µL different concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6] was added into 4.0 mL of the diluted Si QDs solution, and the mixture was shaken completely. Under excitation wavelength at 390 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after shaken 30 s. The fluorescence intensity (FL intensity) was determined at 460 nm. The concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6] was calculated by working curve. The selectivity of the Si QDs probe towards K4[Fe(CN)6] was conducted after addition of inorganic ions solution or biomolecules solution in place of K4[Fe(CN)6] in the same way. All experimental procedure was conducted at room temperature.

A portable UV lamp (365 nm) and a mobile phone with a color scan software were used to take images of the sample solutions for semi-quantitative analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6]. The as-prepared Si QDs solution was diluted 200-fold with 0.04 M B-R buffer solution (pH 2.0) for collected fluorescence signal. The color depth of obtained images was transferred into digital signal by the mobile phone and realized the semi-quantitative determination.

All of table salts and salted food samples were purchased from a local supermarket (Lanzhou, China). Commercial table salt samples, i.e., deep well rock salt, low sodium salt, algae iodate salt with calcium contained, rock salt, were dissolved with ultrapure water, respectively. Salted food samples, i.e., salted turnip, salted cucumber, and salted lotus sprout, were treated referring to the previous report (Li et al., 2006). Firstly, 30.00 g of each shattered salted foods was weighed and soaked for 5 h and then sample was washed with ultrapure water for many times. The washing solution and the soaking solution were transferred to a 100 mL measuring flask and diluted to the tick mark. The centrifugation of the received solution was conducted in 10 min at 12000 rpm and then, 0.45 µm pore size filter membrane was used for the filtration of the supernatant. The obtained stock solution was stored at 4 °C for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters used in the synthesis of the Si QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal fluorescence properties for the synthetic Si QDs, the silane coupling agent, reductant and synthetic conditions were optimized. First, the effect of different combination of silane coupling agent and reductant on fluorescence quenching efficiency of the Si QDs was studied (Table S1). The results indicated that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the Si QDs prepared using DAMO as silane coupling agent and AS as reductant by K4[Fe(CN)6] was maximum. Therefore, DAMO and AS were selected as materials to prepare the Si QDs.

Subsequently, the synthetic conditions of the Si QDs were optimized. The variation of FL intensity versus reaction temperature was described in Fig. S1A. The results showed the outstanding increase of the FL intensity by raising the temperature up to 80 °C and then declined as the temperature raised. The FL intensity gradually increased for 8 h and then stayed nearly stable (Fig. S1B). As presented in Fig. S1C, the FL intensity varied with the amounts of sodium ascorbate. The maximum FL intensity was obtained when the amount of sodium ascorbate was 2.3 g and almost unchanged with increase of sodium ascorbate dosage. Therefore, 2.3 g was selected as optimal amount of reductant and the Si QDs were synthesized in 8 h of stirring under 80 °C.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was used for confirming the morphologies and size of the as-prepared Si QDs. Fig. 1 A showed the obtained Si QDs were well dispersed. The size of these nanoparticles was about 2.1 ∼ 3.3 nm and the average diameter was 2.5 nm. The size distribution histogram of the as-prepared Si QDs was calculated with 150 particles from TEM image (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1C depicted EDS pattern of the Si QDs, manifesting that the Si QDs contained Si, C, N, and O elements (Zhang et al., 2015). The surface functional groups of the Si QDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1D). The absorption band at 3423 cm−1 was attributed to the NH stretching vibration (Xu et al., 2015). The absorption peak at 2930 cm−1 was corresponded to the CH bond antisymmetric stretching vibration (Ye et al., 2016). The absorption band centered at 1036 cm−1 was assigned to the SiC asymmetric deformation and the absorption band centered at 1086 cm−1 was related to the SiOSi stretching vibration (Han et al., 2017). The relatively broad absorption band at 1411 cm−1 was attributed to the CO vibration (Zhong et al., 2015). The strong signal at 1639 cm−1 was result from the CO stretching vibration (Zhong et al., 2015). Moreover, the absorption band at 1325 cm−1 was attributed to the deformation vibration of OH (Zhong et al., 2012), the enhanced sharp absorption at 1259 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of CNH (Ma et al., 2018). The signal around 901 cm−1 showed the stretching vibration of SiH bond (Yi, Deng, Zhang, Li, & Yao, 2013). The peak at 771–796 cm−1 was result from the wagging vibration of NH (Han et al., 2017). Above results showed that the surface of Si QDs was rich in amino and hydroxyl groups which make Si QDs more stable and soluble in water. Excellent water-solubility of the Si QDs could promotes the interaction between the Si QDs and K4[Fe(CN)6] in aqueous solution.

Furthermore, the surface composition and elemental analysis of the obtained Si QDs were further measured via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 A exhibit five major peaks at 532.4, 400.4, 285.2, 154.0, and 102.4 eV, which were assigned to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Si 2s, and Si 2p, respectively (Han et al., 2017). The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2B) exhibited five fitted peaks which were associated with CO (288.5 eV), COH/COC (286.9 eV), CN (285.9 eV), CC/CC (284.8 eV), and CSi (284.0 eV) groups (Han et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2016). The three fitted peaks at 400.9, 400.0, and 399.2 eV in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) were ascribed to N (C)3, CNC, and NSi groups, respectively (Han et al., 2017). The three fitted peaks at 535.9, 532.3, and 531.4 eV in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) were associated with SiO, COH/COC, and CO groups, respectively (Ma et al., 2016). The three fitted peaks at 102.8, 102.2, and 101.5 eV in the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 2E) were ascribed to SiO, SiN, and SiC groups, respectively (Han et al., 2017). The surface constitution of the Si QDs determined by XPS was consistent with the FT-IR results.

The optical properties of Si QDs were studied by UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectrum (Fig. 2F). It was observed that there were two absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectrum (curve c) of the Si QDs. An absorption band at 298 nm was corresponded to the π-π* transition of the CC bond (Jiang, Wang, Meng, Cheng, & Zhu, 2015). Furthermore, there was a broad absorption band around 350 nm, attributed to the transition of n-π* of the Si QDs (Yue et al., 2016). The FL emission peak of the Si QDs was observed at 460 nm (curve b) under excited at 390 nm (curve a). Under UV irradiation, the aqueous solution of the Si QDs exhibited blue fluorescence. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield of the synthesized Si QDs was ∼3.0%. The FL emission spectra of the Si QDs at different excitation wavelengths (360–420 nm) were determined (Fig. S2). It showed the Si QDs posses a typical behavior of excitation- dependent emission, which was the same as most fluorescent nano-materials (Ge et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018; Shangguan et al., 2017). This behavior was ascribed to the optical selection of various size nanoparticles (quantum effect) and various surface state disturbing the band gap of the Si QDs (Dong et al., 2013).

The effect of high ionic strength in solution, different temperature and pH values, and the continuous strong light exposure on the FL intensity of the obtained Si QDs were investigated. The FL intensity of Si QDs in different concentrations of NaCl was investigated and as shown in Fig. S3, it was stable even at 1000 mM, showing the outstanding salt tolerance due to Na+ and Cl− neither complex with amino or hydroxyl groups on the surface of the Si QDs, nor can induce aggregation of the Si QDs by electrostatic interaction. As displayed in Fig. S4A, the FL intensity of Si QDs almost did not change when the temperature was raised from 20 to 90 °C, which manifested that the Si QDs exhibited good thermostability. Moreover, the FL intensity of the Si QDs gradually weakened as the pH value increased from 2.0 to 12.0 in Fig. S4B. This pH-dependent fluorescence property was attributed to the change in surface charge of the Si QDs by protonation-deprotonation (Dong et al., 2013), which could be proved by the pH dependence of the zeta potentials of the Si QDs (Fig. S4C) (Feng et al., 2016). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the obtained Si QDs remained stable after light illumination for 60 min under 390 nm (Fig. S4D), confirming that they have superb anti-photobleaching capability. This property ensured the accuracy of the determination result of proposed method for K4[Fe(CN)6] and showed the Si QDs could be used in long-term bio-imaging and sensing application. All these results indicated that the as-prepared Si QDs have potential applications in several fields, especially in sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for K4[Fe(CN)6]
ce:section.ce:para: The detection conditions were optimized for obtain a high sensitivity. The pH value of sensing system was the first key factor. The FL intensity of the Si QDs revealed an excellent quenching after addition of K4[Fe(CN)6] in the pH range from 2.0 to 4.0 (Fig. S5A). The quenching efficiency was maximum when the pH value was 2.0. This phenomenon may be caused by the highest positive zeta potential of the Si QDs under the acidic condition (pH = 2.0), and the electrostatic interaction between the Si QDs and K4[Fe(CN)6] reached its maximum point (Table S2). Hence, for further detection, 2.0 was chosen as the optimal pH value of the sensing system. Moreover, the response time of the Si QDs to K4[Fe(CN)6] was researched. The FL response was completed within 30 s when the Si QDs and K4[Fe(CN)6] solutions were mixed (Fig. S5B), Table S3 showed the response time of other methods was 10–120 times than our present method. Thus, the 30 s was selected as the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response property of the Si QDs toward K4[Fe(CN)6]
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the performance of this detection system was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, after the addition of various concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6] into the Si QDs solution (0.04 M B-R buffer, pH 2.0), a quite distinct fluorescence quenching was observed. Fig. 3B showed a significant linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency
F 0
F
and the concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6]. The linear regression equation was
F 0
F
± 0.034 =
1.010 ± 0.013
+
0.2018 ± 0.0034
c
K 4
[Fe
CN
6
]
(μg/mL), with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.996 and a linear range of 0.05–8.0 μg/mL. Compared to the reported methods, the linear range of the method was wider (Table S3). Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 30 ng/mL based on
3 σ / k
(
σ
is the standard deviation of the control, and
k
is the slope of the calibration curve) (Pan et al., 2017). The data of the working curve was supplied in the Supplementary materials (Table S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the Si QDs for K4[Fe(CN)6] detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity of the Si QDs for the identification of K4[Fe(CN)6] was conducted. Interference effect was studied from other cations (
K
+
,
NH 4
+
,
Ca
2
+
,
Fe
2
+
,
Mg
2
+
,
Ba
2
+
,
Mn
2
+
,
Pb
2
+
,
Al
3
+
,
Hg
2
+
), anions (Ac−,
SO 4
2 -
,
SO 3
2 -
,
NO 2 -
,
NO 3 -
,
PO 4
3 -
,
IO 3 -
,
I
-
,
HCO 3 -
,
CO 3
2 -
), and some small molecules (glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), cysteine (Cys), lactic acid (LA), phenylalanine (PHE), tyrosine (Tyr)) in table salt and salted food. As shown in Fig. 3C and D,
K
+
,
IO 3 -
, and
I
-
(commonly exist in salt) hardly produced distinct interference for K4[Fe(CN)6] detection even at 10-fold concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6]. Fig. 3C and D also demonstrated that the interference from other ions and small molecules could be neglected. Subsequently, the anti-interference effect of the Si QDs was also studied by adding a mixture containing of K4[Fe(CN)6] and other potential interferences (ions or small molecules) into the sensing system. Almost no significant influence was observed compared to that observed when only K4[Fe(CN)6] existed. These results revealed that the Si QDs possess excellent selectivity and robust tolerance for K4[Fe(CN)6] sensing. This method meets the selective requirements and is suitable for detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] in food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible FL response mechanism of the Si QDs to K4[Fe(CN)6]
ce:section.ce:para:
As demonstrated in Fig. S6, the addition of K4[Fe(CN)6] induced the aggregation of the Si QDs, which may be caused by some intermolecular interactions such as electrostatic or hydrogen bonding interactions. Due to K4[Fe(CN)6] did not contain the group which could form hydrogen bonding with the Si QDs, it could be presumed that there may exist the force of electrostatic interaction. In order to prove the point, the measurement of zeta potentials was performed. Fig. S7 displayed that the zeta potentials of K4[Fe(CN)6] and the Si QDs were −6.55 mV and 5.76 mV, respectively. When K4[Fe(CN)6] and the Si QDs solution were mixed, the zeta potential of mixture solution became 0.845 mV, which suggested that the binding between the Si QDs and K4[Fe(CN)6] was caused by electrostatic interaction, owning to protonation of the Si QDs and ionization of K4[Fe(CN)6] under the detection condition (pH = 2.0). Based on the dissociation constant of H4[Fe(CN)6] (
p
K
a 1
< 1
,
p
K
a 2
< 1
,
p
K
a 3
=
2.22
,
p
K
a 4
=
4.17
) (Li et al., 2007), it can be deduced that K4[Fe(CN)6] exists as
H 2
Fe(CN) 6
2 -
in the sensing system. The above results indicated that electrostatic interaction induced aggregation of the Si QDs, thus cause the fluorescence quenching of the Si QDs. Furthermore, in order to prove that the fluorescence quenching of the Si QDs results from K4[Fe(CN)6] rather than coexist anions, a series of measurements of zeta potential were performed. As depicted in Fig. S8, K4[Fe(CN)6] possessed more abundant negative charges than the coexist anions, indicating that the electrostatic interaction between K4[Fe(CN)6] and the Si QDs is stronger than that of coexist anions and the Si QDs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching from these coexist anions could be neglected.

Furthermore, there scarcely overlapped between the excitation and emission spectra of the Si QDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of K4[Fe(CN)6] (Fig. S9), indicating that inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) as possible detection mechanism were ruled out (Gao, Ye, Cui, & Zhang, 2012; Kong et al., 2017; Lu, Quan, & Xu, 2017; Xiao et al., 2018).

After excluding the FRET and IFE, the remaining effects of fluorescence quenching were researched. Generally, FL can also be quenched via static quenching effect (SQE) or dynamic quenching effect (DQE) (Zhai, Wang, Yu, Wang, & Mao, 2014). Both SQE and DQE through the ground-state complex formation model could be theoretically demonstrated by the Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (1) (Shamsipur et al., 2018).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
Q
where
F 0
and F are the steady-state FL intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively;
K SV
is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6]. In this sensing system,
F 0
F
= 1.010 + 0.2018
c
K 4
[Fe
CN
6
]
was the linear relationship between the FL intensity ratio
F 0
F
of the Si QDs and the concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6] and it was in accord with Stern-Volmer equation. Lifetime quenching efficiencies
τ 0
τ
and UV–vis absorption spectra of the Si QDs under different concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6] were researched by Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy and Ultraviolet spectroscopy to distinguish static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. Where,
τ 0
and
τ
were the FL lifetime of the Si QDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6], respectively. Quenching was deemed to be dynamic when
F 0
F
=
τ 0
τ
, while static quenching was considered when
F 0
F
≠
τ 0
τ
(Arkin, Stemp, Turro, Turro, & Barton, 1996; Feng et al., 2016). As exhibited in Fig. S10 A and B, no significant quenching lifetime was detected after addition of various concentrations of K4[Fe(CN)6], that was to say
τ 0
τ
=
1
and
F 0
F
≠
τ 0
τ
. Additionally, the absorbance peak of Si QDs was changed around at 350 nm after added K4[Fe(CN)6] (Fig. S10C). Accordingly, the fluorescence quenching of the Si QDs derived from SQE (Zu et al., 2017). In order to ensured the sensitivity and operation convenience of the proposed method, room temperature should be chosen as the best reaction temperature in SQE. From Table S5, It manifested that all photoluminescence decays required three component exponential fit, which implied the existence of multiple de-excitation processes (Li et al., 2017). From the above explanations, the Si QDs were induced to aggregate via electrostatic interaction, which resulted in the statically quenching of the fluorescence of Si QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in actual samples
Semi-quantitative analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] by mobile phone
ce:section.ce:para: This assay was further predigested by using a portable UV lamp as the excitation source and a mobile phone as the readout. A weaken of the blue fluorescence of the mixed solution was distinguishable by the naked eyes when the concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6] was above 20 μg/mL (Fig. S11). The fluorescent signal could be collected by a mobile phone and further semiquantified by a color scan software. Therefore, this method is suitable for rapid detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] in resource-limited regions where expensive and sophisticated equipments are absent. The workers could quickly establish a curve for analyzing K4[Fe(CN)6] in food by utilized a hand-held UV lamp and a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other application of the Si QDs
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned previously, FL intensity of the obtained Si QDs solution gradually weakened with the pH increased. As shown in Fig. S12A, bright fluorescence of the Si QDs can be observed at low pH values, while only relatively weak emission can be recorded at high pH values. More importantly, good linearity (R 2 = 0.997) could be found between the FL intensity of Si QDs and the pH value in the range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. S12B). The pH response of the Si QDs could be explained by these surface groups of the Si QDs occurred by protonation-deprotonation (Liu et al., 2017). The phenomenon of pH dependent fluorescent property indicated that the Si QDs could be applied as a pH-monitoring sensor. The further research using fluorescent Si QDs monitoring pH in different cells is still ongoing in our laboratory.

In brief, the water-soluble Si QDs were synthesized by simple one-step strategy and used as a fluorescent probe for K4[Fe(CN)6] sensing. The as-prepared Si QDs possessed unique properties including thermostability, salt tolerance and anti-photobleaching. Moreover, this method of detecting K4[Fe(CN)6] based on the fluorescent Si QDs shows many fascinating properties, such as environmental-friendly, time-saving, wide linear range, Outstanding selectivity, portable feasibility and easy operation. Significantly, the fabricated fluorescent probe has been successfully used for the analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6] in some table salts and salted food samples, and the probe combined with a mobile phone were applied in Semi-quantitative analysis of K4[Fe(CN)6]. Furthermore, the Si QDs probe also was used to monitor pH with a fairly wide range from 2.0 to 12.0. We believe that the established method will have great potential application not only in food chemistry but also in other industrial fields.
None.
The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675068).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.02.111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
